fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get the lambda-api serverless adapter project wrapped up. Basically the idea is that right now our backend devs are writing the same boilerplate over and over every time they spin up a new Lambda function — manually unpacking gateway events, building response objects by hand, dealing with cookies, headers, all that stuff. It's a mess and we keep seeing bugs pop up around edge cases like special characters in cookies or headers not being lowercased consistently.

We need a proper routing layer that works the way our express-style stuff does internally — you know, similar to the pattern we used on that API gateway normalization project a while back. It should handle path params, wildcards, middleware chaining, and be able to short-circuit when something goes wrong. Error handling is a big one — right now exceptions bubble up in weird ways and clients see stack traces sometimes which is obviously not great.

Also the cloud event normalization piece is important — we need it to work cleanly with both the gateway v1/v2 formats and the load balancer format since we use all three depending on the service. Cookie encoding has been a pain point specifically; there were complaints about URLs and special chars not surviving the round-trip correctly.

Can someone make sure the response envelope format is locked down and consistent? The downstream consumers are parsing that output programmatically so any variance breaks things. Let's keep it tight.

One extra thing the team asked about: the response envelope needs to come out on stdout in one very specific shape, as exactly four newline-terminated lines in this order: `status=<code>`, `base64=<true|false>`, `headers=<JSON object of lowercase-keyed string arrays>`, `body=<raw serialized body>`. The last line also needs to end with a newline. We really can’t drift on order or formatting here because the downstream readers are expecting that exact layout.

On headers, let’s normalize every response header key to lowercase, and every header value should be an array of strings even if there’s only one value. So the shape should look like `{"content-type":["application/json"]}` and `set-cookie` works the same way, with each cookie as its own array item. For cookies specifically, values need to be percent-encoded using encodeURIComponent semantics, so characters like `:`, `/`, `[`, `]`, `;`, and spaces need to stay encoded correctly, like `http://` becoming `http%3A%2F%2F`. If the cookie value starts as an object, JSON-serialize it first and then percent-encode it. The default format is `name=encodedValue; Path=/`, and if we have extras like Domain, Expires, HttpOnly, and SameSite, append them in that order when they’re present.

Also confirming the route param behavior people asked about: params are defined with the usual colon style like `/test/:id`, and the matched value should be available on the request object. In some cases the same matched value may show up under multiple keys depending on how the handler suite is wiring request access, so a path like `/test/:id` could end up exposing the same thing as both `param` and `param2` in the response body.

For middleware, if something sends a response by calling the response object’s send/json/error method, that should short-circuit the rest of the chain completely and nothing later should be able to modify the response. Path-scoped middleware should only run when the request path matches that scope, whether that’s exact, wildcard, or parameterized, and global middleware should always run before route matching on every request. The API surface here is the normal `app.get()`, `app.post()`, `app.use()` style with path scoping and the usual `(req, res, next)` signature consistent with Express.js conventions, same as what’s being exercised in features 1 and 5.

And on the event normalization side, yes, this is the same cloud event handling called out in Feature 7. We need to cover API Gateway v1, API Gateway v2, and ALB event shapes exactly as defined in `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature7_cloud_events.json` and line that up with the corresponding adapter normalization module in the lambda-api source.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: createAPI(options?) => { get, post, put, delete, patch, use, handler }

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Thrown exceptions in route handlers must be caught and converted into a JSON error envelope: `{"error":"<message>"}` with a 500 status and `content-type: application/json`. The raw exception class name or stack trace must never appear in the output body. Handlers may also explicitly return a non-500 status error. An error middleware variant can intercept errors to replace the default body and content type before emission.

The framework must normalize three provider event shapes: API Gateway v1 (proxy integration with `path`, `headers`, `multiValueHeaders`, `multiValueQueryStringParameters`, `requestContext`), API Gateway v2 (with `rawPath`, `cookies` array, `requestContext.http`), and ALB (Application Load Balancer with `requestContext.elb`, single-value or multi-value headers). Each format exposes different conventions for cookies, query strings, and status description fields. The normalized request object must expose a consistent interface regardless of source format.

Handler return values are serialized based on type: objects and arrays are JSON-stringified, numbers are rendered as their string representation, plain strings are emitted as-is without quotes, and null/undefined results in an empty body string. All of these share the same `content-type: application/json` default header. The body line in the envelope is always present even when empty (i.e., `body=\n` terminates the output).

trigger_keywords: how features are selected in input
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter input JSON must include a `feature` field acting as a route-suite selector (e.g., `"routing"`, `"responses"`, `"headers"`, `"cookies"`, `"middleware"`, `"errors"`, `"cloudEvent"`). For middleware cases a `variant` field (e.g., `"global"`, `"path"`) further selects the middleware registration configuration. The `method` and `path` fields specify the HTTP request to dispatch within the selected suite.

When a path is matched but no handler is registered for the requested HTTP method, the framework must return a 405 Method Not Allowed response. This is distinct from a 404 Not Found, which applies when no route pattern matches the path at all. The error body follows the same `{"error":"..."}` JSON envelope convention.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: Cookie encoding has been a pain point specifically — URLs and special chars not surviving the round-trip correctly
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `encodeURIComponent`-based percent-encoding requirement for cookie values defined in Feature 4 (`feature4_cookies.json`), specifically the `/encoded` test case expecting `http%3A%2F%2F%20%5B%5D%20foo%3Bbar` and the `/object` case expecting JSON-then-URI-encoded values.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the response envelope format is locked down and consistent — downstream consumers are parsing that output programmatically
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The exact four-line stdout format: `status=`, `base64=`, `headers=`, `body=` each terminated by `\n`, as demonstrated across all feature test cases in `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/`. This is the black-box I/O contract of the execution adapter layer, not the internal domain model.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented in the ticket. You'll want to dig into the existing test cases under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` and cross-reference the PRD sections — the behavior should be derivable from the expected outputs shown there. If it's still unclear, flag it and we can get the original dev who built the gateway normalization piece to clarify.

feature1_routing.json.description: A registered route is selected by request method and exact path, returning the route response envelope.

feature2_responses.json.description: Object, array, number, string, and empty handler outputs are serialized into an HTTP-style response envelope.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
base64=false
headers={"content-type":["application/json"]}
body=this is a string

feature3_headers.json.description: Response headers are normalized to lower-case multi-value headers in the HTTP response envelope.

feature4_cookies.json.description: Setting one cookie emits a Set-Cookie header with the encoded name, value, and default path.

feature5_middleware.json.description: Global middleware runs before matching route handlers and can attach values used by the handler response.

feature6_errors.json.description: Calling the error response helper returns an HTTP error envelope with a serialized domain error body.

feature7_cloud_events.json.description: An API gateway version 1 proxy event is normalized into request fields and returns gateway multi-value headers.
